Trump says Iran must move quickly on nuclear talks as oil market reacts

By John Smith | April 4, 2026

President Donald Trump said on Friday that Iran would face serious consequences if negotiations over its nuclear programme failed, increasing pressure ahead of expected diplomatic contacts in Oman.

"They have a choice to make, and they need to make it fast," Trump said during remarks to reporters. "We want an agreement, but we are prepared for other outcomes if necessary."

The comments came as regional tensions remained elevated around the Strait of Hormuz, a strategic shipping route that has seen repeated security warnings in recent weeks.

Iranian officials signalled that Tehran was open to indirect dialogue, but rejected what they described as coercive language from Washington. A senior diplomat said Iran would defend its national interests and would not accept restrictions beyond its treaty obligations.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio said diplomacy was still the preferred route, adding that "there is still a path forward if both sides are serious." He said the United States was consulting closely with European and Gulf partners.

Energy markets reacted swiftly to the latest rhetoric. Brent crude traded around the $90 mark as traders assessed the risk of further disruption to shipping and exports in the region.

Analysts said any prolonged instability in the waterway could add further pressure to global energy prices because a large share of seaborne oil trade moves through the strait.

European governments urged restraint, while Omani intermediaries continued efforts to keep communication channels open between Washington and Tehran.
